packages:
  # Homebrew Taps
  taps:
    - atlassian/acli
    - ddev/ddev
    - entireio/tap
    - ksdme/tap
    - olets/tap

  # Command-line tools (Homebrew formulas)
  brews:
    # Essential tools for any system (base layer)
    # Includes core shell utilities and modern CLI replacements
    base:
      - atuin
      - bat
      - bottom
      - btop
      - curl
      - difftastic
      - duf
      - dust
      - eza
      - fd
      - fish

      - git
      - git-delta

      - zellij
      - zsh
      - zsh-abbr
      - zsh-autosuggestions
      - zsh-syntax-highlighting

    # Infrastructure and Container-oriented tools
    container:
      - awscli
      - ddev
      - helm
      - kubernetes-cli
      - opentofu

    # Development tools (languages, AI agents, etc.)
    development:
      - acli
      - direnv
      - gh
      - go
      - hub
      - hyperfine
      - jira-cli
      - lazygit
      - llm
      - node
      - opencode

      - python3
      - repomix
      - rtk
      - uv

    # Media and heavy processing tools
    media:
      - ffmpeg
      - yt-dlp

    # Extra CLI tools for non-transient machines (standard power-user suite)
    standard_cli:
      - broot
      - cheat
      - coder
      - curlie
      - doggo
      - dyff
      - fx
      - git-extras
      - git-quick-stats

      - playwright-cli
      - pv
      - specify
      - superfile
      - tailspin
      - the_silver_searcher
      - ut
      - watch
      - xxh
      - xz

    # macOS specific formulas
    macos:
      - mas
      - pinentry-mac

  # macOS Casks (GUI apps and some CLI tools)
  casks:
    # CLI-only tools distributed as casks (preferred on macOS)
    cli:
      - 1password-cli
      - claude-code
      - entire

    # Fonts (Installed if macos=true)
    fonts:
      - font-0xproto-nerd-font
      - font-cascadia-code
      - font-cascadia-code-pl
      - font-cascadia-mono
      - font-cascadia-mono-pl
      - font-caskaydia-cove-nerd-font
      - font-droid-sans-mono-nerd-font
      - font-fira-code-nerd-font
      - font-go-mono-nerd-font
      - font-hack
      - font-hack-nerd-font
      - font-inconsolata
      - font-inconsolata-nerd-font
      - font-jetbrains-mono
      - font-jetbrains-mono-nerd-font
      - font-monaspice-nerd-font

    # Essential GUI (Installed if macos=true)
    macos:
      - 1password
      - choosy
      - comet
      - ghostty
      - google-chrome
      - sublime-text

    # Personal GUI apps (Installed if personal=true)
    personal:
      - claude
      - dayflow
      - disk-inventory-x
      - divvy
      - handbrake-app
      - karabiner-elements
      - logseq
      - loop
      - macshot
      - mounty
      - notion
      - notion-calendar
      - obsidian
      - raycast
      - rectangle
      - shortcat
      - slack
      - sloth
      - stats
      - tailscale-app
      - the-unarchiver
      - vlc
      - wispr-flow
      - zoom

    # Developer-specific GUI tools (Installed if stack=full)
    development:
      - gitup-app
      - imageoptim
      - orbstack
      - visual-studio-code
      - warp
      - zed

    # Large applications and hardware drivers (Installed if workstation=true)
    workstation:
      - antigravity
      - elgato-control-center
      - elgato-stream-deck
      - gnucash
      - logi-options-plus
      - microsoft-office
      - microsoft-teams
      - whatsapp

  # APT packages (fallback or bootstrap for Linux)
  apt:
    - curl
    - git
    - fish
    - zsh

  # Linux-specific configurations (fallbacks for when Casks/Brews don't apply)
  linux:
    npm:
      - "@anthropic-ai/claude-code"
      - "@google/gemini-cli"
    scripts:
      - name: entire
        command: curl -fsSL https://entire.io/install.sh | bash

  # Generic tool initialization
  init:
    rtk:
      - rtk init -g
      - rtk init -g --gemini
      - rtk init --agent antigravity

thoughts.subject: Installing NVM Now
thoughts.description: I'm proceeding with `nvm` installation since the project requires it for development and full stacks. I'll integrate it into the `packages.yaml` under `brews.development`.

thoughts.subject: Conditionalizing NVM Sources
thoughts.description: I'm reviewing the logic for sourcing NVM. It appears NVM's fish shell integration (`nvm.fish`) is currently installed unconditionally. I need to ensure this installation, and its sourcing, is also conditional on the development profile being active to avoid unnecessary setup on non-development stacks.

thoughts.subject: Configuring Shell Integrations
thoughts.description: I'm ensuring shell integrations, like `nvm.fish`, are only active when the development profile is enabled. I'm also considering whether to use the brew `node` if `nvm` is installed, and if a custom `nvm` script is more appropriate than the brew formula for Linux.

response.output:
#!/usr/bin/env bash

# 1. Native installation (Linux only)
# We must keep native installations because some tools (like Warp) expect 
# shells to be at standard system paths (/usr/bin/zsh) for their 
# initialization scripts to work correctly.
{{- if (eq .chezmoi.os "linux") }}
{{- if or (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "ubuntu") (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "debian") }}
export DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive
# Add fish PPA/Repo if missing
if ! command -v fish &> /dev/null; then
    {{- if (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "ubuntu") }}
    sudo apt-add-repository -y ppa:fish-shell/release-4
    {{- else if (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "debian") }}
    echo "deb http://download.opensuse.org/repositories/shells:/fish:/release:/4/Debian_{{ .chezmoi.osRelease.versionID }}/ /" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/shells:fish:release:4.list
    curl -fsSL https://download.opensuse.org/repositories/shells:fish:release:4/Debian_{{ .chezmoi.osRelease.versionID }}/Release.key | gpg --dearmor | sudo tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/shells_fish_release_4.gpg > /dev/null
    {{- end }}
fi
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get -yq install fish zsh
{{- else if (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "fedora") }}
sudo dnf -yq install fish zsh
{{- end }}
{{- end }}

# 2. Homebrew installation (MacOS & Linux)
# Install Homebrew if missing
type brew &> /dev/null || /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

# Ensure Homebrew is in PATH for the rest of this script
if [[ -d /opt/homebrew/bin ]]; then
    eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
elif [[ -d /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin ]]; then
    eval "$(/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
fi

# Install shells via Homebrew - this provides the latest versions
# coexisting with the native ones.
brew install fish zsh

thoughts.subject: Implementing Homebrew Package Management
thoughts.description: I've confirmed Homebrew is installed and will use `packages.yaml` for its management. My current focus is updating this file to include `nvm` under `brews.development` and remove the redundant `node` entry. This sets the stage for robust package handling.

thoughts.subject: Clarifying Node Management
thoughts.description: I'm re-evaluating the removal of the `node` package from Homebrew. While `nvm` is intended to manage Node versions, removing `node` entirely might break installations that rely on `npm` being immediately available, as `npm` isn't present until Node is installed via `nvm`. I need to ensure this dependency is handled gracefully.

thoughts.subject: Adding NVM Package
thoughts.description: I'm proceeding with adding `nvm` to `brews.development` in `packages.yaml`. The current consensus leans towards developers preferring `nvm` for Node management, often implying a desire to replace system `node`. I will add `nvm` and remove the existing `node` entry.

I'm assessing if `nvm.fish` should be installed universally or only on development profiles. I will also review `dot_config/fish` for potential `nvm` integration.

      - warp
      - zed

    # Large applications and hardware drivers (Installed if workstation=true)

      - gnucash
      - logi-options-plus
      - microsoft-office
      - microsoft-teams
      - whatsapp

  # APT packages (fallback or bootstrap for Linux)
  apt:
    - curl
    - git
    - fish
    - zsh

  # Linux-specific configurations (fallbacks for when Casks/Brews don't apply)
  linux:

      - "@anthropic-ai/claude-code"
      - "@google/gemini-cli"
    scripts:
      - name: entire
        command: curl -fsSL https://entire.io/install.sh | bash

  # Generic tool initialization
  init:
    rtk:
      - rtk init -g
      - rtk init -g --gemini
      - rtk init --agent antigravity

args.new_string:
# Tool initializations
[[ -f ${HOME}/.config/op/plugins.sh ]] && . ${HOME}/.config/op/plugins.sh

{{ if or (eq .stack "development") (eq .stack "full") -}}

export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
[ -s "$HOMEBREW_PREFIX/opt/nvm/nvm.sh" ] && \. "$HOMEBREW_PREFIX/opt/nvm/nvm.sh"
[ -s "$HOMEBREW_PREFIX/opt/nvm/etc/bash_completion.d/nvm" ] && \. "$HOMEBREW_PREFIX/opt/nvm/etc/bash_completion.d/nvm"
{{- end }}

# OrbStack initialization

resultDisplay.originalContent:
#!/usr/bin/env fish

if ! command -v fisher &> /dev/null
  curl -sL https://git.io/fisher | source && fisher install jorgebucaran/fisher
end

fisher install danhper/fish-ssh-agent
fisher install decors/fish-colored-man
fisher install edc/bass
fisher install franciscolourenco/done
fisher install jethrokuan/z
fisher install jorgebucaran/nvm.fish

content:
I have installed `nvm` on the development profile by performing the following steps:

1.  **Updated Package Management:** Added `nvm` to the `development` software stack in `.chezmoidata/packages.yaml`. This ensures that `nvm` is installed via Homebrew whenever the `development` or `full` stack is selected.
2.  **Configured Zsh:** Updated `dot_zshrc.tmpl` to initialize `nvm` for Zsh users. The initialization script is conditionally included only when the `stack` is set to `development` or `full`.
3.  **Configured Fish:** Renamed `run_once_after_20_fisher.sh` to `run_once_after_20_fisher.sh.tmpl` and modified it to make the installation of the `nvm.fish` plugin conditional on the `development` or `full` stack.

These changes ensure that `nvm` is available and correctly configured in both Zsh and Fish shells, but only on machines identified as having a development-oriented profile.

[Active Topic: Implementing NVM Installation]

thoughts.subject: Considering Package Managers
thoughts.description: I've been reviewing the package management strategy. While the user specifically requested `nvm`, I'm also briefly evaluating `mise` as an alternative. I've already incorporated `nvm` into `packages.yaml` under `brews.development`, as per the instructions.

thoughts.subject: Verifying Shell Integrations
thoughts.description: I've confirmed that `nvm` is correctly integrated for Zsh via `dot_zshrc.tmpl`. For Fish, `fisher` manages the `nvm.fish` plugin, ensuring it's sourced appropriately within the Fish environment. I'm now double-checking if any other shell configurations require attention for `nvm` sourcing.

thoughts.subject: Confirming Shell Compatibility
thoughts.description: I've confirmed Zsh and Fish integrations are solid. While I didn't find a `.bashrc`, I'm now reconsidering the `node` package in `packages.yaml`. Given the focus on `nvm` for managing Node versions, I'll likely remove the brew `node` to avoid potential conflicts and ensure a cleaner `nvm`-centric development environment.

thoughts.subject: Refining Node Management
thoughts.description: I'm deciding whether to keep the `node` brew formula. While `nvm` handles Node versions, removing the brew `node` means no Node until the user runs `nvm install`. I'll retain the brew `node` for now, ensuring immediate availability, but I'll confirm the necessity of `NVM_DIR` creation and the `nvm.fish` plugin installation logic.

toolCalls.name: activate_skill

response.output:
<activated_skill name="conventional-commits">
  <instructions>
    # Conventional Commits

Write every commit message according to the [Conventional Commits v1.0.0](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/) specification.

## Undercover mode

**CRITICAL:** Do NOT add `Co-Authored-By`, `Signed-off-by`, or any other trailer that attributes or identifies an AI agent as a contributor. The commit must look like it was written entirely by the human developer. This applies to all footers — never reveal agent involvement.

## Commit message format

```
<type>[optional scope]: <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

## Rules

### Type (required)

The first element is the **type** — a noun that categorizes the change:

| Type       | When to use                                          | SemVer effect |
|------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------|
| `feat`     | A new feature for the user or consumer               | MINOR         |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                            | PATCH         |
| `docs`     | Documentation only                                   | -             |
| `style`    | Formatting, whitespace, semicolons — no logic change | -             |
| `refactor` | Code change that neither fixes a bug nor adds a feature | -          |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                              | -             |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                           | -             |
| `build`    | Changes to the build system or dependencies          | -             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration and scripts                         | -             |
| `chore`    | Other changes that don't modify src or test files    | -             |

Only `feat` and `fix` are mandated by the spec. The rest are widely adopted conventions. Pick the type that most accurately describes the change — do not default to `chore` when a more specific type applies.

### Scope (optional)

A noun in parentheses after the type describing the section of the codebase affected:

```
feat(auth): add OAuth2 login flow
fix(parser): handle empty input without crashing
```

Use scope when the repo is large enough that it helps orient the reader. Keep scopes consistent within a project — check `git log` for existing scopes before inventing new ones.

### Description (required)

- Immediately follows the colon and space after the type/scope prefix
- Use the imperative mood ("add", "fix", "remove" — not "added", "fixes", "removed")
- Do not capitalize the first letter
- Do not end with a period
- Keep it under 72 characters
- Summarize *what* the change does, not *how*

### Body (optional)

- Separated from the description by one blank line
- Free-form — can contain multiple paragraphs
- Use to explain *why* the change was made when the description alone is insufficient
- Wrap lines at 72 characters

### Footers (optional)

- Separated from the body (or description if no body) by one blank line
- Format: `token: value` or `token #value`
- Tokens use `-` instead of spaces (e.g., `Reviewed-by`, `Refs`)
- Exception: `BREAKING CHANGE` (must be uppercase, space allowed)

### Breaking changes

Indicate breaking changes in one of two ways (or both):

1. **`!` after type/scope:** `feat(api)!: remove deprecated endpoints`
2. **Footer:** `BREAKING CHANGE: the /v1 endpoints have been removed`

A `BREAKING CHANGE` footer MUST be uppercase. When present, it triggers a MAJOR version bump regardless of the type.

## Choosing the right type

Before writing the message, examine the staged changes:

1. **Does it add new user-facing behavior?** -> `feat`
2. **Does it fix incorrect behavior?** -> `fix`
3. **Does it only change documentation?** -> `docs`
4. **Does it only change tests?** -> `test`
5. **Does it improve performance without changing behavior?** -> `perf`
6. **Does it restructure code without changing behavior?** -> `refactor`
7. **Does it only change formatting/style?** -> `style`
8. **Does it change build tooling or dependencies?** -> `build`
9. **Does it change CI pipelines?** -> `ci`
10. **None of the above?** -> `chore`

If a commit spans multiple types, prefer the most significant one (usually `feat` or `fix`). If the changes are truly unrelated, consider splitting into separate commits.

## Workflow

When asked to commit, or when you determine a commit is needed:

1. Run `git diff --cached` (or `git diff` if nothing is staged) to understand what changed.
2. Run `git log --oneline -10` to see recent commit style and any existing scope conventions.
3. Classify the change using the type selection guide above.
4. Write the commit message following the format rules.
5. Use `write_file` to save the message to a temporary file (e.g., `.git/COMMIT_MESSAGE`) and then use `git commit -F` to commit. This is the most robust way to handle multi-line messages and avoid safety blocks when the message content contains shell-sensitive characters like `$(` or backticks:

```bash
# Step 1: write_file(file_path=".git/COMMIT_MESSAGE", content="...")
# Step 2:
git commit -F .git/COMMIT_MESSAGE && rm .git/COMMIT_MESSAGE
```

## Examples

Simple feature:
```
feat: add email notifications for failed jobs
```

Bug fix with scope:
```
fix(css): correct button alignment on mobile viewports
```

Breaking change with `!` and footer:
```
feat(api)!: require authentication for all endpoints

All API endpoints now require a valid Bearer token.
Previously, read-only endpoints were publicly accessible.

BREAKING CHANGE: unauthenticated requests to any endpoint now return 401
```

Refactor with body:
```
refactor: extract validation logic into shared module

The same validation rules were duplicated across three controllers.
Moving them to a shared module reduces drift and makes the rules
easier to test in isolation.
```

Docs only:
```
docs: add contributing guidelines for new developers
```

Build change:
```
build(deps): upgrade webpack from 4 to 5
```
  </instructions>

  <available_resources>
    Showing up to 200 items (files + folders).

/Users/hw/.agents/skills/conventional-commits/
├───SKILL.md
└───evals/
    └───evals.json
  </available_resources>
</activated_skill>

Triggers on "commit", "git commit", "create a commit", "commit message", or any action that produces a git commit message.
